So, a large part of why we continue to maintain Django Evolution, instead of relying on Django 1.7's migrations, is because we develop Review Board, and this also depends on Django 1.6 (again, for many many _many_ compatibility reasons -- each new release of Django introduces substantial breaking changes, which is bad for us and our customers).

Whatever decision impacts Django Evolution will no doubt impact Review Board as well (assuming Review Board is being packaged in Debian).

Option #1 would do nothing other than create confusion at this point. We _do_ want to ship a build that provides build-time compatibility with Django 1.6 through 1.9, but we're not ready to do that yet. Having one in the wild would likely just create support issues for us (when you're dealing with database migrations, it's crucial that these things work before they get into others' hands).

Option #2 might be your only option for now, which is really a shame.

What about Option #3: Don't include an actual dependency on a Django package. Make sure the package description works only with Django 1.4 through 1.6. If someone is installing Django Evolution. If a package needs it, then it's likely using Django 1.6 anyway. If a developer needs it, they're also handling their Django dependency anyway. It's highly unlikely any project out there is using Django Evolution as a gateway to their Django dependency (which would be dangerous and unpredictable).

This way, the package can remain in Debian, without having dependency issues. When we're able to ship properly tested support for modern Django, the description can be updated, but there otherwise wouldn't need to be any special changes made on your end (such as re-introducing the package).

@ibnesayeed I would prefer smart defaults. If what "looks like" an IPFS hash is passed, treat it as so and process accordingly. I currently have a very fundamental case of this with reading in abs/rel CDXJ files for the replay system. This also stinks of some potentially dangerous scenarios.

That said, allow special flags to force the type of interpretation would be good. Maybe that should be the initial approach but there is something elegant about specifying `ipwb replay myIndex.cdxj` over `ipwb replay -f myIndex.cdxj` or `ipwb replay -i <ipfs hash>`.

> We discussed this yesterday and the idea of separating out all of the encryption attributes into their own JSON object, potentially contained within the record.

Keep that in mind I provided that example above which is essentially a vertical split of the index, replicating the concept of database normalization. There are many ways to envision this. There could be an encryption identifier that can be used as the lookup column of the key file and recorded as a reference in the index. Using `true` as a value is one way to indicate that the record is encrypted and encryption information can be found in a provided key. This will be needed to identify those encrypted records from plain ones if the index has both, and this approach is only desired when there is only one way of providing keys, i.e., using a key file. Alternatively, the current option to embed that detail in the index itself can continue to work (we can certainly wrap related attributes in a single object).

> How about if a client were offered the option to provide a key.cdxj file for interpreting the mementos available for replay?

If the replay server is intermediary and does not have access to keys directly, then I would not encourage this approach of a user uploading keys file to it for replay because: 1) now the server needs to maintain session for each user and keep those keys forever for every single user every connected, 2) the server makes sure there is isolation, so keys of one user are not being used by another, and 3) the server now has access to private information of the clients which it did not have before.

If for whatever reason the client is the only one who has access to keys and the replay server is nothing but a proxy to fetch stuff from IPFS network, then the best option is to store keys in the browser itself and decrypt on the client-side (somewhat like end-to-end encryption). ServiceWorker can be helpful in this case where you perform decryption operation right before serving it to the browser.

So `sascfg.ini` is just what I'm calling a config flat text file. 

The way to picture it I think is this:

Right now configuration takes place inside a script which is executed by python. The script uses python syntax and objects (dicts, strings) to define ways of connecting. They exist at the module level in `saspy.sascfg.dict_name`. To change the defaults, you would edit the actual python script.

The alternative is that there is still a `sascfg.py`, but it doesn't get edited. When it gets imported, it simply reads in a flat textfile that is formatted according to a certain specification. It then assigns the same variables at the module level (`saspy.sascfg.dict_name`) based on the contents of the config file. In addition, there is a script run at install-time that generates this config file (called `sascfg.ini` perhaps) with reasonable defaults based on the environment in which it's installing.

1. Reason for my first commit ( `.encode('utf-8')` ):
By converting `str` to `bytes`, it just **suppresses** the issue, doesn't really **solve** it. I just did it to handle passing any strings _with encoding declaration_ in future. 2. Reason for my second commit (removing encoding declaration):
This actually **solves** the issue by following what `lxml.etree.fromstring` wants. You may not have this commit but then you are not following `lxml`'s guidelines.

The problem is that you do not have enough samples, only 2. The filtering method is very restrictive to control the fdr (=percentage of irrelevant added features) for any distribution and dependency structure.

If you do not have enough statistics (=number of samples) the feature filtering will treat the features as unimportant because it could be that observed correlations only occur by chance. For really small sample sizes almost no features will be extracted.

It is not clear what the actual error is, as Turbodbc is not telling you the true reason the command failed. It tries to get more information on the failure, but it fails to do so. Instead you get this unfortunate error message that won't help you much in solving the problem.

You can try turning on the unixODBC trace functionality (see for example https://www.easysoft.com/support/kb/kb00945.html) which should record the native error message. This will have a severe performance impact so it may not be very useful. It may also write a very large log to disk, so please take this into account.

> 1. Why do we need prefix replacement at all? I'm afraid I have not find any explanation on that - could you, please, point me into the right direction?

During the build process of certain bits of software (Unix-first stuff mostly, often stuff built using Autotools, but CMake-built software is not exempt from this either) record paths they are told about. These paths are used in the C or C++ code to load auxiliary files (or whatever), but of course they won't exist on the end user's machine so we must re-write them. It's not good to do this though and I sometimes try to make the software dynamic in this regard instead.

> 2. ..

@kalefranz, can you answer this, seems like a fair point but I would guess it's more complicated than that?

From my perspective, I can make this problem go away for Qt (and probably will do), by fixing the perms on Qt and making sure it doesn't suffer binary prefix replacement if not necessary, but that's on the anaconda-issues side of things.

Re-opening for further discussion, cheers.

This is awesome information!  I don't have a big package cache to run against, and I didn't feel like faking one out, so really appreciate this profiling work.  Let me chew on it a bit...

FYI if you want to watch debug output, use two `-v` flags.  Three `-v` flags and you get "trace" level output, which is *extremely* verbose, especially during install/uninstall operations.

For the get_index part of the time, that concerns downloading and local caching repodata.json.  If there isn't a local cached copy, then you should see HTTP 200 responses, repodata downloads, and pickle writing output in debug logs.  That scenario eats up a bit of time.  However those repodata files don't change that often, at least for repo.continuum.io--maybe a few times a day.  So for successive runs, get_index should become pretty speedy, with seeing either no HTTP calls at all (going off the mtime of the local cached file) or HTTP calls with 304 responses, and then only reading pickled data structures into memory.  This part is as optimized as it's going to get in the near-term.  In the longer term, we're looking at alternate ways of storing and accessing this information (e.g. a sqlite db) so that it can be more efficiently queried and we don't have to load the whole universe of information into memory for each and every operation.

All the regex time is a surprise though, and seems like something we should be able to improve dramatically.

Perhaps skip the nested transaction scheme and just go with something like this (adapted from the go version)?

```
def _txn_retry_loop(conn, callback)
    with conn.begin():
        conn.execute("SAVEPOINT cockroach_restart")
        while True:
            try:
                v = callback(conn)
                conn.execute("RELEASE SAVEPOINT cockroach_restart")
            except sqlalchemy.exc.DatabaseError as exc:
                if isinstance(exc.orig, psycopg2.OperationalError):
                    # See https://www.cockroachlabs.com/docs/transactions.html and
                    # https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroach-go/blob/master/crdb/tx.go#L85
                    ambiguous_error = 'CR000'
                    # Error code '40001' from
                    # https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/static/errcodes-appendix.html#ERRCODES-TABLE
                    retryable_error = psycopg2.errorcodes.SERIALIZATION_FAILURE
                    if exc.orig.pgcode in [ambiguous_error, retryable_error]:
                        conn.execute("ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT cockroach_restart")
                        continue
                raise
```

As you no longer use them, you can then raise exceptions when the user attempts to use nested transactions.

Also, notice how the go plugin (and hence my adaptation thereof) issues a single `SAVEPOINT cockroach_restart` instruction only, whereas the current python implementation issues just one on entry to every iteration of the retry loop.

Hi @RobertoPrevato, thanks for reaching out! Design discussions are always welcome. 😄 

The usage of `time.sleep` was very much intentional, as we want to make sure the thread is suspended while we wait for the next try. I believe it is a fairly normal thing to do in a synchronous programming model. In addition, the user can specify a different retry policy to shorten the time to wait(even to 0 if needed) if they feel like it is not the behavior they desired. Please share your thoughts if you think there is a better way of doing this!

I do agree that the asynchronous programming model is far superior in terms of efficiency, and that is why we will break ground on the official async version of the SDK later this year. It will feature a new design similar to the [new Go SDK](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-blob-go), and use Swagger generated protocol code.

This can happen when the page blob is heavily fragmented. Unfortunately increasing the timeout doesn’t always help because the maximum timeout enforced by the service for this operation is not large enough to handle very high fragmentation. Instead, you need to request the page ranges over a subset of the blob, and compose the results.

The problem and solution are detailed (for .NET) in this blog post:
https://blogs.msdn.microsoft.com/windowsazurestorage/2012/03/26/getting-the-page-ranges-of-a-large-page-blob-in-segments/


Here is a Python sample (based off the .NET sample)  to give you an idea of how you can compose the page ranges over subsets/chunks of the blob.

```
def get_page_ranges_diff_chunked(self, start_range, end_range, range_chunk_size, container_name, blob_name, previous_snapshot):
        range_size = end_range - start_range + 1
        if range_size % 512 != 0:
            raise ValueError("Range size must be in a valid 512 byte interval alignment.")
        
        # Now 'iterations' will be the amount of calls to 'get_page_ranges_diff'
        # Evaluation of the [start_range, end_range] are chunked with subsets defined by 'range_chunk_size'
        iterations = range_size / range_chunk_size  
        start_offset = start_range
        page_range_diffs = []
        for i in range(iterations):
            end_offset = start_offset + range_chunk_size - 1
        # Include your desired parameters.
            page_ranges = self.bs.get_page_ranges_diff(
                container_name, blob_name, previous_snapshot, None, start_offset, end_offset)
            page_range_diffs.extend(page_ranges)
            start_offset = start_range + range_chunk_size
        return page_range_diffs
```

But this rubber-ducking helped me!! Given that there was nothing left, I scoured my environment, and found this:

```
denali[~]> env | grep -i brow

```

This is a carryover of my `bashrc` from my Ubuntu days, but on OSX calling `google-chrome` doesn't do anything.  So what was happening in my case was that the browser command was failing, leaving me without a browser.

This shows another failure mode that can affect an unsuspecting user: if for whatever reason they don't get the browser to open, they're left to read startup logs (something a new user isn't necessarily always adept at).

I recently had the opportunity to help a user who was a complete newbie to Python/Jupyter, and it was really satisfactory to be able to get them to go from zero to working notebook with plots in the course of a short session (and it got them excited about the possibilities).  But this person had never interacted with a terminal at all nor had any real experience programming. This library is new for me and TBH I don't understand why it separates parsing errors and validation errors. I would love to hear why this design decision was made. Here's an example:

```python
from schematics.models import Model
from schematics.types import IntType, DateType
from schematics.types.compound import ListType, ModelType


class ObjectModel(Model):
    id = IntType(min_value=0, required=True)
    date = DateType(required=True)


class CollectionModel(Model):
    data = ListType(ModelType(ObjectModel), min_size=1, required=True)


def test1():
    """Passes."""
    return CollectionModel({'data': [
        {'id': -1},
        {'id': 10}
    ]})


def test1_1():
    """Fails with::

        ModelValidationError: {'data': [
            {'date': ['This field is required.'],
             'id': ['Int value should be greater than 0.']},
            {'date': ['This field is required.']}
        ]}

    """
    model = test1()
    model.validate()


def test2():
    """Fails with::

        ModelConversionError: {'data':
            {'date': ['Could not parse aaa. Should be ISO8601 (YYYY-MM-DD).']}
        }

    """
    return CollectionModel({'data': [
        {'id': -1, 'date': 'aaa'},
        {'id': 10}
    ]})


def test2_1():
    """Fails because of test2() failure.
    """
    model = test2()
    model.validate()
```

The only thing that changes is date field for one of the nested objects - but see how behaviour changes in this case. Instead I would expect test2() to pass and test2_1() to fail with validation error like:

```
{'data': [
    {'date': ['Could not parse aaa. Should be ISO8601 (YYYY-MM-DD).'],
     'id': ['Int value should be greater than 0.']},
    {'date': ['This field is required.']}
]}
```

Another thing that confuses me. Models allow 2 ways of passing values to them - pass raw data in `__init__` and by setting attributes. Raw data example is above. And here's how model behaves when one is setting attribute values:

```python
collection_model = CollectionModel()
# hm, no ModelConversionError (parsing error)
collection_model.data = [{'id': -1, 'date': 'aaa'}, {'id': 10}]
collection_model.validate()
```

This snippet raises

```
ModelValidationError: {'data': {'date': ['Could not parse aaa. Should be ISO8601 (YYYY-MM-DD).']}}
```

So previously that was `ModelConversionError`, and it wasn't possible to create model with incorrect values types (I believe that's the main purpose of parse/validation errors separation) - now it raises different error type and model does contain wrong value types before validation step.

And even more interesting example:

```python
object_model = ObjectModel()
object_model.date = 'aaa'
object_model.id = -1
object_model.validate()
```

Raises

```
ModelValidationError: {'date': ['Could not parse aaa. Should be ISO8601 (YYYY-MM-DD).'], 'id': ['Int value should be greater than 0.']}
```

In contrast to `test2()` here `ModelValidationError` mixes both validation errors ('Int value should be greater than 0.') and type errors ('Could not parse aaa. Should be ISO8601 (YYYY-MM-DD).').

So this confuses me a lot - I would prefer to have consistent behaviour and here it looks like model parsing/validation behaves differently based on model structure and how one fill the model with data.

I'm about to start merging backward-incompatible features into the main branches, and therefore Schematics v1 will move on to live on a separate legacy branch. It can still be maintained, but after v1.2.0 the main focus will be on moving things forward by dealing with the current backlog of PRs and issues, most of which are more or less backward-incompatible.

Before I let the branches diverge, I'd like to get all the non-breaking, non-controversial PRs out of the way. I've already merged a few contributions, but then there are these:

* #349 and #350 I consider to be bugfixes unless someone can explain otherwise. These I'm planning to incorporate in v1, so a review would be appreciated.

* #355 introduces functionality that should exist according to documentation. It will only break things in what I believe to be a rare case, so I'm thinking it could go in v1 as well.

* #351 (912afcc02c5506290d8fe5d818207c141cc6d1e3) changes the primitive value from `None` to `{}` when a model collapses into nothing when all its fields are empty (`None` or `{}` or `[]`) and `serialize_when_none` is `False`. __Avoid Sub-Projects__
While looking at this yesterday I noticed that we were uploading folders containing projects into one DukeDSProject. For example `CSHL : Bacterial pathogenesis 2012` seems like it should be it's own DukeDSProject. This will make sharing just one project with another user possible. 

The initial check to determine what files to upload was taking forever. This is due to the time it takes to request folder children and file hashes from DukeDS. It takes between 0.25 seconds to 1.5 seconds to list the contents of a folder. While getting the hash for each file takes 0.25 seconds to 0.8 seconds.

You should create a script to upload each project folder into it's own DukeDSProject. This will reduce the time spent asking DukeDS for directory contents and file hashes.

__DukeDSClient is not Dropbox__
The use case you are trying to achieve (as I understand it) is to function similar to dropbox.
This is out of the scope of the initial development done on DukeDSClient.
There has been no work to support users modifying files while they are being uploaded.
There is no mechanism for keeping track of which files have been uploaded.
All of the commercial implementations of this I have seen run as a service. 
ddsclient (as of now) is meant to run from the command line and exit.
You are more than welcome to contribute changes to meet this need.

__Forthcoming Changes__

2. `cd librdkafka && ./configure && make && make install && ldconfig`

One problem here is that the semantics might be slightly unclear when you pass a naive datetime. I suppose we can just interpret naive times as local time.

So I've been thinking about this a bit and how it would be implemented. I created a [little test script](https://gist.github.com/pganssle/74a7b5dfae366211956e) which implements an rrule DAILY with a BYMONTH argument.

The way I see it, BYXXX where the frequency of XXX is less than (or equal to) the frequency of the rule is essentially a filter, while if XXX is greater than the frequency of the rule is a product. I was hoping that we could easily use `itertools.ifilterfalse()` for the filter version, so my test script implements DAILY with a BYMONTH argument using a filter (`test_filter()`) and by using a generator which intelligently skips to the next valid argument (`test_skip()`). Unfortunately, the results are not encouraging:

> Why do you want to throw exception?

Exceptions aren't bad at all. `api_id` **must** be an integer, and the caller is responsible to ensure such thing. It's just as easy to add the `int()` call when passing the parameter. This way they're also directly responsible for the cast to possibly fail. Any reason why the cast should be done inside and not when calling the constructor?

> As I use same session for both clients

You shouldn't do that. The clients are not aware of each other and there may be synchronization issues, when reading or saving the session file for instance. If you need two connections, you should use `.create_new_connection()` instead.

You *could* try to pass the `client1.session` as the session for `client2`. That might help with the synchronization but I'm not sure how Telegram will behave.

> What is in general proper approach to connect to same Telegram user from two processes, threads? 

`TelegramClient.create_new_connection()`

> Should each work with different session names (and thus files)?

They would be completely independent, which is probably not what you intend.

@bgruening Yep, in case of ambiguity or unparsability the ID field can be used.

A key difference between CWL and Galaxy tool definitions is that we only run one command within a single CWL tool description.

The common Galaxy idiom of many commands is **not** possible in CWL without a separate wrapper. This was done on purpose and I think it is the source of some confusion in this discussion.

That command may be a script with its own dependencies, but you can't `bowtie-build foo; bowtie bar` without wrapping that in a separate script which would need to be packaged in some way (Docker, conda, ...). At that point you're already doing packaging so I see no reason to include dependency management beyond tool ID and any optional enhancements.

That clipping bug was fixed in leaflet a long time ago. Your issue is a malformed rectangle b/c you forgot to close it. This should work (note the repetition of the first point that closes the box):

```python
import folium

lat, lon = (44, -93)
m = folium.Map(
    location=(lat, lon),
    zoom_start=9
)

coords = [
    [-93.2, 43.8],
    [-92.8, 43.8],
    [-92.8, 44.2],
    [-93.2, 44.2],
    [-93.2, 43.8],
]

line = {
    'type': 'Feature',
    'geometry': {
        'type': 'LineString',
        'coordinates': coords
    }
}

folium.GeoJson(
    line,
    name='line',
    style_function=lambda x: {
        'color': '#999999',
        'fill': '#999999',
        'opacity': 0.8,
        'fillOpacity': 0.5}
).add_to(m)

m
```

To prevent errors like that you can use the `Rectangle` class (where you give the bounds and leaflet will do the rest):

```python
m = folium.Map(
    location=(lat, lon),
    zoom_start=9
)

bounds = [
    [44.2, -93.2],
    [43.8, -92.8],
]
    

folium.Rectangle(
    bounds,
    color='#999999',
    fill='#999999',
    opacity=0.8,
    fill_opacity=0.5,
).add_to(m)

m
```

Seems very straight forward. I don't know how common this use case is, but if you're using it than that should be enough :)

Hi Sasank,
thank you for your feedback. There are a couple of ways in which you can split an experiment into different files. A very basic hack is to import a function into your experiment file and turn it into a captured function. Something like this: 
``` python
from utils import my_function
my_function = ex.capture(my_function)
```

But of course this won't lead to well modularized code, since the configuration for `my_function` is still part of your experiment file and you will need to keep the naming consistent in both files. 

If I understand your usecase correctly, it is precisely what I intended [`Ingredients`](https://sacred.readthedocs.org/en/latest/ingredients.html) to solve. At this point they are not very well documented and a bit beta, because I'm still figuring out how exactly they should behave. 

The basic idea of an `Ingredient` is to be a reusable part of an experiment. You can define them in separate file, and they have their own configuration and captured functions. But they can be added to an experiment and then their configuration will be part of the experiment config. 
Maybe you could give them a shot and see how they work for you. I'd be very happy to get some feedback on them, so please let me know if they solve your problem and about any issues or unexpected behaviours you encounter.  Thank you.

Z3 performs the rewrite:
    (bvsdiv x y) 
into
    (ite (= y 0) (bvsdiv_0 x) (bvsdiv_I x y))

So you can extract Boolector friendly semantics by 
replacing bvsidiv_i by bvsdiv and (bsdiv_0 x) by (bsdiv x 0) for suitable bit-widths.

Z3 does not pretend to have any guarantees of producing SMT2 compliant output as it also supports several non-standard extensions beyond these.

> In one of our libraries for reinforcement learning we use a generic function to clone a lasagne network with or without keeping the params.

Interesting to see that this works -- so basically, `copy.deepcopy` is able to recreate a network?

> is there any way to implement functionality of applying network parts multiple times in Lasagne?

There's one solution that you haven't mentioned: Construct your network such that it processes both parts at once, and split them up afterwards. I.e., instead of:
```python
out1 = lasagne.layers.get_output(my_nn, {my_nn_input:input1}
out2 = lasagne.layers.get_output(my_nn, {my_nn_input:input2}
```
do:
```python
input = T.concatenate((input1, input2), axis=0)
...
l_out1 = lasagne.layers.SliceLayer(my_nn, slice(None, len(input1)), axis=0)
l_out2 = lasagne.layers.SliceLayer(my_nn, slice(len(input1), None), axis=0)
```
Now you can continue constructing your network, and don't have to worry about collecting parameters from the different fragments. This will also result in a more efficient solution, since Theano can process both input parts in a single minibatch. However, it won't work well for recurrent networks if the two input parts have large different sequence lengths (the shorter one would have to be padded).

> if our clone_network can fit Lasagne spirit

It would be nice to have such functionality, but we'll have to investigate whether `copy.deepcopy` really does what we need or whether we would have to extend the `Layer` interface in some way to allow cloning in all cases. But in general, yes, this would be a nice addition if it's easy to do.

----

Thinking a little more, another solution would be a special layer class that embeds the application of a network to its input(s):
```python
class MacroLayer(MergeLayer):
    def __init__(self, incomings, network, input_layers=None, **kwargs):
        super(MergeLayer, self).__init__(incomings, **kwargs)
        self.network = network
        all_layers = lasagne.layers.get_all_layers(network)
        if input_layers is None:
            input_layers = [layer for layer in all_layers if getattr(layer, 'input_layer', None) is None]
        self.input_layers = input_layers
        self.params = dict(itertools.chain.from_iterable(layer.params.items() for layer in all_layers))
    def get_output_shape_for(self, input_shapes):
        return lasagne.layers.get_output_shape(self.network, dict(zip(self.input_layers, input_shapes)))
    def get_output_for(self, inputs, **kwargs):
        return lasagne.layers.get_output(self.network, dict(zip(self.input_layers, input_shapes)), **kwargs)
```
Each branch of your network would become a `MacroLayer` then, each linked to its own `InputLayer` instance, but both containing the same `network` for their computation steps. `lasagne.layers.get_all_layers()` wouldn't expand the `MacroLayer`, but you could still retrieve all its parameters.

I agree.  It's very easy to add a BidirectionalLayer layer.  I've been using this in my own experiments:

```python
def BidirectionalLayer(layer_class, *args, **kwargs):
    kwargs.pop('backwards', None)
    l_fwd = layer_class(*args, backwards=False, **kwargs)
    l_bck = layer_class(*args, backwards=True, **kwargs)
    return ElemwiseSumLayer([l_fwd, l_bck])
```

So just to through out something I did over the weekend, which the main goal was restructuring the Lasagne Recurrent implementation. Nevertheless, the root of all evils of making a single "Step" Layer was the fact that some layer has single input/output some multiple.

So what I did was remove the `MergeLayer`, and make the `Layer` to have `input_shapes` and `input_layers` (plurals) only. E.g. if you pass a single think it converts it to a tuple. Additionally, all layers now have `get_ouput_shapes` and `get_ouputs_for` (again plurals) which always return lists/tuples. This way everything is uniform across the board - everything accepts and outputs multiple things. Added extra flag `max_inputs` to `Layer` and then it checks in the base `__init__` that the input_shapes are not more, in which case raise an error. Then for back portability `get_output_shape` returns `get_output_shapes()[0]` if the len is 0 otherwise throws an error. Same for `get_output`. All Layers had to change the return of shapes and outputs to tuples, which is just adding an extra comma. Had to modify some of the tests to accommodate the changes in the `Mock` passed, but now all tests pass. Everything works and the recurrence layer is close to the CustomRecurrent layer, but much easier to extend for StepLayers and I have something like this:
```
class GRUStep(AbstractStepLayer):
    def __init__(self, incoming, num_units,
                 nonlinearity=nonlinearities.tanh,
                 gates_function=nonlinearities.sigmoid,
                 W=init.Orthogonal(),
                 h_init=init.Constant(0.),
                 **kwargs):
        super(GRUStep, self).__init__(incoming, 3 * num_units, **kwargs)
        if len(self.input_shapes[0]) != 2:
            raise ValueError("GRUStep accepts only 2D inputs")
        self.W = self.add_param(W, (num_units, 3 * num_units),
                                name="W")
        self.add_init_param(h_init, (None, num_units), name="h_init")
        self.f = nonlinearity
        self.g = gates_function

    def get_output_shapes_for(self, input_shapes):
        x_shape = input_shapes[0]
        return (x_shape[0], self.num_units // 3),

    def get_outputs_for(self, inputs, **kwargs):
        x = inputs[0]
        h = inputs[1]
        n = self.num_units // 3
        # If we precompute the output this is only dot(h, w)
        a = T.dot(h, self.W)
        ru = self.g(a[:, :2*n] + x[:, :2*n])
        r = ru[:, :n]
        u = ru[:, n:]
        c_in = a[:, 2*n:]
        c = self.f(c_in * r + x[:, 2*n:])
        return u * c + (1 - u) * h,
```
I'm pretty sure you probably don't want so radical change, but hey I really enjoyed it, and now things are way more simple to manipulate around. And the whole recurrent module with 2 more layers implemented is 500 LOC.

I'm on the fence as to whether we want to change the default behaviour. I guess it's more natural, but as you point out it can also be unstable. Torch and TensorFlow both seem to prefer having the network output log-odds, even when paired with cross-entropy. In Torch you put a "LogSoftmax" on top of it, in TF you just use the "softmax_crossentropy_with_logits" loss function or its variants, which takes care of things.

For beginners this is probably less straightforward, but maybe it's something that people are getting used to?

A related concern is that this potential instability introduces a failure case that will be hard to understand precisely for these beginners, so maybe we're not doing them a favour by changing this default after all...

Anyway, as I said, I'm on the fence, I think I have no strong opinion either way. Just something worth thinking about :)

There are two contexts in which your question makes sense:

1. If you're using Veritesting (http://angr.io/api-doc/angr.html#angr.exploration_techniques.veritesting.Veritesting), it does loop unrolling as an optimization to reduce branching. You can control how the loops are unrolled by passing the `loop_unrolling_limit` argument to the underlying analysis (https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/analyses/veritesting.py#L167).

2. If you're doing normal symbolic execution, you can use the LoopLimiter (http://angr.io/api-doc/angr.html#angr.exploration_techniques.looplimiter.LoopLimiter) technique to discard paths that are looping more than you want them to. You can also write your own techniques to do this fairly easily. Unfortunately, there doesn't seem to be docs for this at the moment. I opened https://github.com/angr/angr-doc/issues/93 to remind ourselves to get to these when have a moment :-)

Hi,

Can you enable debug logging for ncclient.transport.ssh?

Note that in RFC 6241 (https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6241#section-3) we see:

  All NETCONF messages MUST be well-formed XML, encoded in UTF-8
  [RFC3629].  If a peer receives an <rpc> message that is not well-
  formed XML or not encoded in UTF-8, it SHOULD reply with a
  "malformed-message" error.  If a reply cannot be sent for any reason,
  the server MUST terminate the session.

First step would be to use the debug logging suggested above to determine if the returned data is UTF-8-compliant. If it isn’t, the device vendor may need to comment.

Cheers,

> I checked and it seems like libpcap 1.7.4 does indeed have the `pcap_file` function.

`pcap_file` dates back at *least* as far as libpcap 0.4, so you probably don't even need to check for it, unless you want to be able to support versions of libpcap *prior* to a version released in July 1998.

> The former header is only a wrapper around the inclusion of the actual `pcap.h` header located in `pcap/pcap.h`, which means that when we look inside of it for the `pcap_file` function we don't find it and end up not defining `HAVE_PCAP_FILE`.

Yes - do not rely on `pcap.h` in the top-level include directory being *the* include file declaring the functions.  We created the `pcap` directory to hold additional libpcap header files, and put `pcap.h` there, with a top-level `pcap.h` file left for source compatibility (unlike, for example, some versions of Red Hat, which moved it and *didn't* leave behind anything for source compatibility).

I've been reading about pipes etc. and my worry now is that pipes have a maximum buffer size that they can hold. This means that if the running process tries to write more data than that to the pipe (e.g. a large environment or a lot of output from a python script)it will hang indefinitely. This is why it was implemented as a concurrent thread so it could consume the pipe as it was fed.

I realized that real pattern matching (`~`) is very slow compared to `LIKE` in Postgres.

The need of pattern matching is limited to:

Permission backend:
* delete every access control entries that starts with a parent id `/parent/%`
* obtain accessible objects in a plural endpoint `^/object/uri/([a-Z0-9-_])+$`

Storage backend:
* delete all and purge deleted with parent id that starts with `/parent/%`
* get_all  with parent id that starts with `/parent/%`

So the only use case we have for pattern matching is the `get_accessible_objects()` where we cannot use `LIKE /parents/%` because we don't want to include children (see bug #965). Using `SIMILAR TO` in PostgreSQL appears to be more efficient than POSIX regexps, but does not support the whole range of regexp operators. 

So finally, I suggest that we stick to `*` instead of being able to pass POSIX regexps, since it would imply a big loss of performance. See #974.

Closing, please reopen if you think I miss a point!

SR300_AUTO_RANGE family of options is different from standard options. 
For once, the firmware is expecting to accept new values to all AUTO_RANGE options as a bulk, and it makes little sense to assign values to them one by one. In addition, it is very hard to determine the visual effect of each individual parameter. Intel Algo team has defined a set of suggested combinations, refered in librealsense as **ivcam_presets**. 
Here is a list of presets, with each preset being is optimized for a specific usage:
ShortRange     
LongRange             
BackgroundSegmentation
GestureRecognition    
ObjectScanning        
FaceAnalytics         
FaceLogin 
GRCursor              
Default 
MidRange              
IROnly                
Please see rsutils.h - **rs_apply_ivcam_preset** for the corresponding values of AUTO_RANGE options. It is not recommended to set AUTO_RANGE options by hand, and the fact that they are exposed in librealsense API as individual options is more of a technical constraint to enable rsutils.h rather then an intentional design decision. It is likely those options will get deprecated in the future, in favor of using presets. (obviously, if you wish to tinker with them you are still welcomed to, librealsense is an open-source)

Just a reminder from all the descriptions of Adapt, it uses a "Known Entity Tagger", so issues that state it is not recognizing items are for the most part "working as designed". 

That being said, numbers are kind of important :)

Numbers as text (i.e. twenty) will vary based on language, and thusly require localization. Adapt itself doesn't currently have a concept of localization (and associated data), so I'm not convinced this belongs within core Adapt right now. 

Numbers as numbers (20) vary less for most languages, and I feel more comfortable pulling them into the framework, but at the same time, they're very easily represented by regular expression entities (\d+). Would a collection of built-in regexes be helpful in this case? The way Adapt uses named group regexes might make this less useful, as the numerals would likely be used in conjunction with other localized phrases (think 20.3 degrees being captured by "?P(<Temperature>\d+\.\d+) degrees")


I'm going to leave this open, but I don't expect to make much progress on it short term. My short term recommendation would be to use a regex that fits your use case.






Another nugget in here is around the case of incomplete intents, which Adapt is not yet prepared to handle. When an intent is determined to be invalid, it's confidence is set to 0.0. In an ideal world, we could leave the confidence alone, and have a concept of validity or completeness as part of the intent. I believe I have a good way to implement this that is backwards compatible, and this is something that is on my backlog.

You can use a PUT with `{"interactions": [...]}`to add multiple requests at the same time. This will effectively do the DELETE and multiple POSTs. The Python API would need to be modified to allow multiple requests to be set up at once. Don't know if you can do this with python, but you can chain requests like this in Ruby:

```python
(pact
     .given('Authenticated')
     .upon_receiving('a request to /login')
     .with_request('get', '/login/UserA')
     .will_respond_with(200, body=expected))
     .given('UserA exists and is not an administrator')
     .upon_receiving('a request for UserA')
     .with_request('get', '/users/UserA')
     .will_respond_with(200, body=expected)
```

Yes, I did consider that possibility. It may be useful to be able to work with more than one agent, and that possibility is left open. That said, making `pulp_smash.settings` smart enough to deal with lists of configurations is a change in semantics that, I think, will introduce some complications.

Code that currently interacts with the `settings.json` file assumes that the file uses a simple labeling system. Want the system running Pulp? Call `ServerConfig().read('pulp')`. Want a system running the Pulp agent? Call `ServerConfig().read('pulp agent')`. What if the requested system isn't available? A `ConfigFileSectionNotFoundError` is raised. Easy enough.

Imagine we want to conditionally run some tests based on how many Pulp agents are available. If we require that a section named `pulp_agents` be present in the config file:

```python
# Require at least one agent
try:
    pulp_agents = ServerConfig().read('pulp agents')
except exceptions.ConfigFileSectionNotFoundError:
    raise unittest2.SkipTest()
if len(pulp_agents) == 0:
    raise unittest2.SkipTest()
```

A bit verbose, but doable. But what happens when we go to save a config?

```python
cfg.save('pulp')  # adds a new entry called "pulp"
cfg.save('my backup')  # adds a new entry called "my backup"
cfg.save('pulp agents')  # overwrites list of pulp agents?
```

Uh oh. We have an issue with semantics here. Do we need to add new methods?

```python
# Append to the list of configs named "pulp agents". Create a new list if no list
# is present, or overwrite the existing server config if one is present.
cfg.append('pulp agents')
# Remove item from a list of configs. Raise an exception if "pulp agents"
# is not present, or is present but not a list, or if no items are left.
cfg.pop('pulp agents')
```

We're greatly expanding how complicated `ServerConfig` is, and the semantics a user needs to be aware of are correspondingly becoming more complicated.

Another solution to this issue is to just state that Pulp Smash expects certain labels to be present, and widely advertise its expectations. For example, here's what I'm proposing right now:

* A section named "pulp" must be present.
* A section named "pulp agent" may be present. If present, tests that require a single agent are run.

If we want multiple Pulp agents in the future, we could state that Pulp Smash expects something else:

* A section named "pulp" must be present.
* Sections named "pulp agent NNN" may be present, where NNN is a decimal number. Tests that require multiple Pulp agents will run if an appropriate number of agents are listed.

Using a labeling system is dumb in some ways. But it has the compelling advantage of letting us avoid complications, both in semantics and implementation.

It's safe in the sense that it won't break anything. The argument for keeping it is that plugins that _don't_ reload (like the embeddings projector) or only reload part of their state (like the graph dashboard) may confuse users who click the "reload" button and expect the state to be…well, reloaded. But one could also say that it's the responsibility of these plugins to alert their users that they're not, in fact, reloading. I don't have any strong opinions; cc @dandelionmane and maybe @dsmilkov ?

> > Under the assumption that the developer already has builddir and srcdir correct in their *_SOURCES variables, that would work and wouldn't require them to get the directories correct again in another place (hotdoc.conf).
>
> Yes, that's why I'm saying that doesn't really address the issue, it's just a proposed workaround that means users still have to "know how to do build systems well".

It solves the issue of ‘I really do not want to have two identical lists of C and H files in different parts of my build system which are going to go out of sync’, which is what I really care about here. I can easily see the following situation happening:
 * Developer adds new C file to project (it may be generated and hence in `builddir`, or it might not be)
 * Developer adds it to `Makefile.am` because otherwise it doesn’t build and they don’t get the functionality they just added
 * Developer forgets to add it to `hotdoc.conf` because there is no immediate feedback about not doing that and it’s another thing to remember when all they really want to do is write the C code
 * Developer finishes off and pushes everything
 * Time passes
 * Someone notices the documentation is broken, and adds the file to `hotdoc.conf`
 * Time passes
 * (If the file is generated:) Someone who is trying to package the code notices that it doesn’t build with `builddir` ≠ `srcdir` any more, and so fixes that in `Makefile.am`. They may not notice that it also needs updating in `hotdoc.conf`

This can all be fixed by using the list of source files from `Makefile.am`, which already exists in every project, has support for `builddir` and `srcdir` which people understand best (if they understand anything about that difference at all), and which developers are forced to keep up to date or none of their code builds.

Note that this could also be solved by somehow having hotdoc detect documentation comments in files which aren’t listed in `c_sources` and warning the user about them, but that still means the developer has to maintain two parallel and identical lists of sources.

Hi @jwhui,

1. For the Native Commissioner API, I think we want to move to a Joiner List approach so we can handle cases beyond a single Joiner.  There would need to be a way to specify Joiner IIDs and multiplex multiple DTLS server sessions.  

Regarding the Joiner API, I'm indifferent on start/stop vs. enable, though pausing the Joiner role with preserved settings allows for coarse power management.  Longer term, I think we'd want some indications to the higher layer to signal candidate networks found and bloom filter hits to allow better management of hunting, and allow Joiner back-off decisions so a device can power management when no candidate networks are present.

2. To the extent the API for the case of an SoC with display is progressing forward with a good plan in mind, adding corresponding NCP hooks is fairly trivial.  We also get the benefit of passing the functional test suite when we preserve some form of a 1:1 mapping.

Setting poll period works and at least for the time being I'll use it, but to me it feels a lot like a workaround, rather than a solution. 
First, because it's somewhat inefficient - CoAP retries with exponential delays, and so would the number of MAC data request frames (in case no CoAP response is received). 
Second, it forces you to constantly control MAC layer from even higher layer. 
Third, this issue may not be obvious and could be not addressed in some applications resulting in higher air-time. 
Fourth, this will need to be addressed in every application (code duplication, increased bug possibility).
On a good side, it gives the flexibility to the user.
I would really like some more thoughts on this.

from xml.dom import minidom
import requests

def GetiAPICookie(url, username, password):
  xml_string="<?xml version=\"1.0\" encoding=\"ISO-8859-1\"?> \
    <ins_api>                         \
    <version>0.1</version>            \
    <type>cli_show</type>             \
    <chunk>0</chunk>                  \
    <sid>session1</sid>               \
    <input>show clock</input>         \
    <output_format>xml</output_format>\
    </ins_api>"
  try:
    r = requests.post(url, data=xml_string, auth=(username, password))
  except requests.exceptions.ConnectionError as e:
    print "Connection Error"
  else:
    return r.headers['Set-Cookie']


def ExecuteiAPICommand(url, cookie, username, password, cmd_type, cmd):
  headers = {'Cookie': cookie}

  xml_string="<?xml version=\"1.0\" encoding=\"ISO-8859-1\"?> \
   <ins_api>                          \
   <version>0.1</version>             \
   <type>" + cmd_type + "</type>      \
   <chunk>0</chunk>                   \
   <sid>session1</sid>                \
   <input>" + cmd + "</input>         \
   <output_format>xml</output_format> \
   </ins_api>"

  try:
    r = requests.post(url, headers=headers, data=xml_string, auth=(username, password))
  except requests.exceptions.ConnectionError as e:
    print "Connection Error"
  else:
    return r.text

def GetNodeDataDom(dom,nodename):
  # given a XML document, find an element by name and return it as a string
  try:
    node=dom.getElementsByTagName(nodename)
    return (NodeAsText(node))
  except IndexError:
    return "__notFound__"


def NodeAsText(node):
  # convert a XML element to a string
  try:
    nodetext=node[0].firstChild.data.strip()
    return nodetext
  except IndexError:
    return "__na__"

Hi @dmitriy-serdyuk, I can see that this is probably pretty frustrating but I don't see a quick fix that has precedence over working out https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/issues/134, especially when that one is likely to change a lot of the server->frontend interactions. 

This has a lot to do with how we're sending the update, namely that we currently "destroy" the old content by [updating it with a new content_id](https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/py/server.py#L414) on an update, forcing [a component update](https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/blob/master/js/PlotPane.js#L27) on the frontend which redraws the whole plot. Possible temporary solutions range from broadcasting updates separately from how new plots are broadcast (thus requiring a separate handler to subscribe to updates) to creating new fields on the server-side that correspond to an update having happened, which then upon the frontend hitting componentShouldUpdate it can undergo different behavior that changes the state (and appends the new values to the existing plot) without redrawing the plot. I'd be happy to accept a PR that implements something along these lines in the meantime while I'm working on 134.

@MarkLalor 
Hi Mark, thanks for the Pull Request!  

First let me attempt to provide a little high level context.  The reason I haven't worried about the current default behavior when hitting Ctrl+C is that I have always implemented a **sigint** handler in all of my ``cmd2`` applications.  But I never wanted to force a default **sigint** handler on end users.  That being said, I think the default behavior of quitting the ``cmd2`` application when Ctrl+C is hit would rarely be desired.

Let me try to answer some of your questions ...

1. It might be slightly better to define the attribute as an instance member inside the ``__init__()`` method, but I think it is fine as it is now as a class attribute.  The only time it would really make a difference is when people are using the SubMenu feature and in that case it is hard to say which alternative would be preferable.  I would even be OK with the attribute having a default value which did not preserve backwards compatibility if @kotfu agrees.

2. As it is, your change can prevent quitting when a ``cmd2`` application is blocking waiting for input from the console.  However, it will NOT prevent quitting while a ``cmd2`` application is executing a command. py::module::import() doesn't happen to call get_internals(). The internals structure is used to track information about types for which pybind11 bindings have been provided and instances of those types, so if you never say class_ you're probably not initializing it. While the internals structure itself is shared among all modules (counting main.cpp and an external pybindings.so as separate modules) that use the same pybind11 version, the reference to it needs to be obtained separately for each module, and looking that up requires the GIL to be held also. The correct fix here probably involves a change to pybind11 that ensures get_internals() is called when creating a scoped_interpreter and when constructing a gil_scoped_release. Calling get_shared_data("") is just a workaround.

                  ${PYTHON_EXECUTABLE} ${PROJECT_SOURCE_DIR}/test.py)

2. In `tests/CMakeLists.txt` replace `--test-command` with `--build-target check` (it must precede `--build-options`).

@erikrose Thanks!. I add not found "[those files](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/tree/master/letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces)" in plain text. That said, yes, the metapackage approach is faulty and I dived in the code of setuptools but there is no checksum verification that's possible so the approach is fraught with perils. 

FWIW, in order to solve a similar proper (e.g. ensure that a Python app of mine always installs with exact versions of dependencies that I vetted all the way) I also used another approach: 

I bundle all the dependencies as wheels or sdists in the repo itself down to pip, setuptools and virtualenv and use a configure script to install this entirely locally, getting everything from a release archive or checkout. 

This is of course a **terribly bad heresy** if this were a library but IMHO **it makes sense for an application** where you want fine and exact control of which deps are installed. IMHO certbot is NOT a library but an application so using a similar approach could allow such a fine control.

You can see the mechanics of this bundling here:
1. the configure [shell script](https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/blob/develop/configure) at the root that handles the bootstrapping (this is for POSIX: there is also a [configure.bat](https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/blob/develop/configure.bat) for Winwoes)...
2. ... which then  delegates to this [configure.py script](https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/blob/develop/etc/configure.py) . This script starts by creating a [virtualenv](https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/blob/develop/etc/configure.py#L172)  and then [installs ](https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/blob/develop/etc/configure.py#L232)requirement [files](https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/blob/develop/etc/conf/base.txt) without calling Pypi, without using a cache or anything external, and optionally run [shell scripts or python scripts](https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/blob/develop/etc/configure.py#L243) organized in a [directory tree](https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/tree/develop/etc/conf) to have various config possible (e.g. devel vs prod, etc) 
3. ... and all the deps are obtained locally from a [thirdparty dir](https://github.com/nexB/scancode-toolkit/tree/develop/thirdparty/prod)

At a high level this is very loosely equivalent in a much simplified way to what certbot-auto does with one major difference: all deps archives are BUNDLED. So I only need to trust (and verify) the integrity of a single release download archive. And since it runs pip, its requirements files could contain SHA256 for verification. And there are fewer moving parts since there is only a single initial download needed.

I am not saying that cerbot should use this script! but I am advocating for the bundling of deps to simplify dealing with application dependencies.

> Got it, thanks for the explanation. Mind if I change the integration
tests to use `-v`?

I think we still want `-vv` on the client side. If you want to write up a PR to run `-vv` on the client side but `-v` for boulder, I'll merge it.

> Also, what is the reason for the `* 10` when keeping track of the
logging level?

The predefined [logging levels](https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html#logging-levels) are given the numeric values 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. By multiplying by 10, one count of `-v` causes you to jump to the next logging level.

> Why do we multiple things by negative?

The way the `count` [action](https://docs.python.org/2/library/argparse.html#action) works in argparse is we start at default value, adding 1 to the result every time the flag is seen. Since we want `-v` to result in a lower numeric logging level, we make the default -3, causing one instance of `-v` to result in a count of -2, which when multiplied by -1 and 10, we get 20 which is the correct logging level (see the logging level link above).

@ohemorange I'm responding to your review comment here, instead of in the thread above because GH's UI isn't working properly for me. 

> if someone's set it up so that multiple lineages use the same archive directory, are we already broken?

I _think_ the answer is yes, already broken, because of how `_update_symlinks` works (which they'd most likely have run after changing a bunch of archive dirs in renewal conf files). Based on our naming conventions, running `certbot update_symlinks` would end in wrong, overlapping things in this scenario.

Can you think of any good reasons as to why we shouldn't treat this scenario as being broken? I suppose it's possible that the user didn't use `certbot update_symlinks` and did everything manually without making mistakes, but this scenario seems unlikely. Also, I don't know if there are other parts of the codebase that rely on the archive dirs not overlapping.

Alternatively, we could also put in a check for this kind of configuration before anything gets deleted? If we find another lineage with an overlapping archive dir, we could just default to not deleting. Could be slow if the user has a lot of certs though, but how often are they going to be revoking certificates anyway?

Maybe we should put a note somewhere in [here](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#modifying-the-renewal-configuration-file) discouraging users from doing this? 

Another idea: we could put a check in the `certificates` subcommand that is like, "hey we noticed you have different lineages using the same archive directory, and that's usually a sign that something might be off".

Yes, these are the commits to feature callbacks for the `transfer_async()` and `register_on_withdrawable_callback()`,
but they probably need a review.
Like it is implied in the original post, once we have specs for a minimal protocol, we should include some default callbacks.
For example the creation of a `on_sucess_()` callback inside of a `transfer_async(..., id)` that posts a `success` message to some websocket/pubsub for that `id` on transfer completion.
There is a implementation hinted for this in my experiments with the WAMP protocol in https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/api/wamp_server.py#L150

I think that a notification for task-completion is desired in most of the current API-methods,
so I propose adding callback functionality for these methods:

- open()
- deposit()
- exchange()
- close()
- settle()

Hey @AdriaanRol thanks for the useful comments. I definitely see your point that the get/set behaviour works well for Instruments, especially because it makes explicit when VISA commands are communicated with the physical instrument. I've made some changes to the ParameterNode that keeps this behaviour and may be a good middle way (see below). The reason why I removed the instrument inheritance from `InstrumentBase` to `ParameterNode` is that the `InstrumentBase` already functioned as a pretty good `ParameterNode`, and so I simply added a few functionalities and replaced the name. I think `ParameterNode` is better suited, since it can be used for things completely unrelated to instruments.

@WilliamHPNielsen Breaking backwards compatibility is definitely an issue, and I've been thinking how to deal with it. I think I've found a solution that works, namely by adding the flag `use_as_attributes` during the `ParameterNode` initialization. In short, it works as follows:

#### Setting `use_as_attributes=False`, which is the default
```
parameter_node = ParameterNode() # use_as_attributes is False by default
parameter_node.new_parameter = Parameter(set_cmd=None)
parameter_node.new_parameter = 42 # Set parameter value works as above
parameter_node.new_parameter(42) # Set parameter value can also be done via brackets like before
parameter_node.new_parameter # Returns Parameter instance.
parameter_node.new_parameter() # Returns Parameter value
```
This is the default behaviour of the ParameterNode, and pretty much behaves the same way as before. Accessing the Parameter attribute of a ParameterNode returns the Parameter, and not its value. 
In this case there are three differences compared to before, which all shouldn't break compatibility:
1. Setting a parameter value can also be done via `parameter_node.new_parameter = 42`. Currently we never set a parameter attribute, and so shouldn't break anything. We can also remove this behaviour if its unwanted.
2. The Parameter name doesn't need to be specified. If unspecified, it will set its name to that of the attribute.
3. Parameters can be added to the ParameterNode (and hence also Instruments) by simply setting its attribute. I also kept `add_parameter` to retain compatibility.

Pretty much all the tests still work, and I don't see why this wouldn't be backwards compatible. Would this be a viable solution? 

#### Setting `use_as_attributes=True`
```
parameter_node = ParameterNode(use_as_attributes=True)
parameter_node.new_parameter = Parameter(set_cmd=None)
parameter_node.new_parameter = 42 # Set parameter value
parameter_node.new_parameter # Returns 42
```
In this case, the `Parameter` attributes in the ParameterNode behave like the values they represent. This is best suited for more abstract classes (meta-instruments) that still need parameter functionality. An example is a Pulse object, whose parameter `Pulse.duration` can be varied in a loop, which in turn will program the instruments to output the pulse for a specific duration. In this case, it is more convenient to treat the parameter as an attribute, as the `Pulse` may have many more attributes, some of which aren't parameters (e.g. `@property` attributes). If getting/setting parameters happens different than other attributes, you would have to remember each time if an attribute is actually a parameter or not. For our group, the ability to access parameters as attributes definitely improves things.

Hi @achercasky sorry for the delay:

I believe you want one of two things:

+ either two Nodes in series (customers queue for the fist node with service distribution `['Uniform', 2, 15]`, then join *another* queue with service distribution `['Normal', 50, 20]`

for this, each node needs an arrival distribution (though the second node will have `NoArrivals`). And also a transition matrix, e.g.:

```
N = ciw.create_network(
    Arrival_distributions=[['Exponential', media], 'NoArrivals'],
    Service_distributions=[['Uniform', 2, 15], ['Normal', 50, 20]],
    Number_of_servers=[1,1],
    Transition_matrices=[[0.0, 1.0], [0.0, 0.0]]
)
```

+ or you have one Node, where the service time consists of two times, the first part sampled from `[['Uniform', 2, 15]`, and the second from  `['Normal', 50, 20]`. For this you will have to create your own distribution and use the `UserDefined` distribution option, e.g.

```
def doubledistribution():
    part1 = random.uniform(2, 15)
    part2 = random.normalvariate(50, 20)
    return part1 + part 2

N = ciw.create_network(
    Arrival_distributions=[['Exponential', media]],
    Service_distributions=[['UserDefined', doubledistribution]],
    Number_of_servers=[1]
)
```

These two options model two very different things, and will produce very different outcomes.

**_Note:_** If using the 1st option Ciw does not support `Normal` distribution, use [`TruncatedNormal`](http://ciw.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Reference/distributions.html#normal-dist) instead. If using 2nd option, ensure that `doubledistribution` does not sample negative numbers (a danger when using a Normal distribution).

I've been looking into this issue for a bit and here is what I found so far:

- On master on my laptop, the script runs in about 35s.
- The sending actor finishes much earlier than the receiving actor, so the benchmark is bottlenecked on the receiving node for most of the job.
- Profiling the receiving node shows that it spends most of its time in `malloc` for redis commands. The main redis commands are for adding to the `TaskLeaseTable` and adding to the `TaskTable`. After taking out writes to the former (which we write to at least as many times as to `TaskTable`), there is no discernible difference in runtime.
- The number of bytes available in the node manager-to-node manager socket blew up as more tasks are sent. This is because the receiving node manager is not reading out tasks fast enough due a known asio issue where a handler only processes 1 message a time from a socket, the same issue that I referenced in my previous comment. A symptom of this was that some of the `ForwardTask` messages (which are written synchronously from one node manager to another) would take >100ms to complete. I modified the node manager to read out more `ForwardTask` messages per handler, but this did not affect the overall runtime. I do think we should incorporate this change into master though. Another thing we could do to reduce the `ForwardTask` latency is to write the message asynchronously, but we would have to buffer the messages ourselves (I haven't implemented this fully, but I think it's worth doing).
- From the above points, i decided that the runtime is mostly going towards the loop between the receiving node manager and actor, which is basically: actor calls `GetTask`, the node manager assigns the actor a task, the actor executes it, and repeat. there is very little that i can see on the node manager side to optimize at first glance, but there may be places on the Python side that we can speed up.
- Since throughput seemed to be bottlenecked by the Python actor, I upped the number of actors to see what the throughput would look like then. With 4 pairs of sending and receiving actors, the job finishes in about 81s.
- The overall throughput of the job is significantly lower than the stable throughput for the receiving actor. For 4 pairs of actors, the overall throughput is 400k/81 = ~5k tasks/s. However, after the first couple rounds, each of the actors sends 10k tasks every 5s, which means a stable throughput of about 40k/5 = ~8k tasks/s. That means there's an unexplained delay of about 30s at the beginning of the job, when the sending actors are still submitting tasks. I haven't looked much into what's happening during this period yet, other than the note about not reading from the socket fast enough.
- After investigating the Python side a bit more, I tried commenting out `ray.put` entirely so that each task on the receiving actor does not put any of its return values in the object store. Obviously this won't work as a general optimization, but just to illustrate what the overhead of `ray.put` is. For a single sending and receiving actor, this increased the stable throughput on the receiving actor from 10k/3s to 10k/1.5s.

So in summary, the main performance issues that I've found so far are:
1. asio handlers need to process more than one event at a time, within some reasonable time limit.
2. `ray.put` adds significant overhead when there are many tiny tasks.

Ok, I think the root of the problem is the same underlying issue in #259 (basically that Python dictionary orderings are non-deterministic). The problem arises as follows.

```python
import pickle

d1 = dict(
    l2coeff=0.005,
    noise_stdev=0.02,
    episodes_per_batch=1000,
    timesteps_per_batch=10000,
    calc_obstat_prob=0.01,
    eval_prob=0,
    snapshot_freq=0,
    return_proc_mode="centered_rank",
    episode_cutoff_mode="env_default",
    num_workers=10,
    stepsize=.01)

s1 = pickle.dumps(d1)
d2 = pickle.loads(s1)
s2 = pickle.dumps(d2)

In terms of implementation, the Arrow format supports [dictionary encoding](https://github.com/apache/arrow/blob/master/format/Layout.md#dictionary-encoding), which basically lets you have multiple references to the same object. Presumably this feature could be used for serializing Python objects with multiple copies of the same internal object.

This should cover most important situations. However, if we do this, there will likely still be some shortcomings. In particular, consider the following.

```python
import numpy as np
import ray

ray.init()

x = np.zeros(3)

def f():
    return x
```

There is only one array involved.

```python
f()[0] = 1
assert x[0] == 1
```

However, suppose we wish to serialize the tuple `(x, f)`. Then we our serialization library will serialize `x` intelligently as an array. However, `f` is a function, which our serialization library will treat as a black box, so we'll fallback to cloudpickle for serializing `f`. When we deserialize things on the other side, the arrays `x` and `f()` will be different.

Ultimate fate declaration.  There is nothing pdfminer.six can do about this.  I spent a lot of time tracking down the problem.  The problem demonstrated in my sample resume pdf provided above is:
The PDF itself provides partial font resources.  Those PDFCIDFont's (Using pdfminer's terminology) provide ToUnicode helper resources (in the pdf itself).  Those ToUnicode resources explicitly and incorrectly map the CID's for 'ff' ligature and 'fi' ligature to '\x00' unicode mappings.

Without ToUnicode mappings provided in the pdfresources you effectively have to OCR the text from rendered output (which is clearly beyond the scope of pdfminer).

The problem in my case is not with pdfminer....it's 100% on the tool that made the pdf and incorrectly mapped the CID's to incorrect unicode values...then "helpfully" provided them in the pdf font resource as the ToUnicode Map.

Getting a traceback is normal – if you write a regular Python script that doesn't use trio and hit control-C, you'll get a traceback, and we try to match what regular Python does when it makes sense :-). (Sometimes trio's tracebacks are annoyingly wordy, which is something we want to fix, but that's not the issue here.)

What's strange here is the `TrioInternalError`. You should have gotten a `KeyboardInterrupt` and a traceback that shows your code, not a bunch of trio internals. It looks like you've hit some kind of bug inside the kqueue I/O manager's cancellation logic. We should figure that out and fix it.

@dstufft Okay, so... let's see if I have this right. You have a bunch of receivers who take in events from some external sources (a bunch of TCP connections). You want to batch up these events, and then once every X seconds or Y events – whichever comes first – you want to submit a whole batch to some other service.

The simplest way to do this would be:

```python
async def sender(q):
    while True:
        batch = []
        # Note that we to put a limit on the whole batch collection process,
        # so we put the loop inside move_on_after
        with move_on_after(X):
            while len(batch) < Y:
                batch.append(await q.get())

        await send_batch(batch)
```

You also asked about what happens if you have multiple `sender`s running. One way would be to use a second queue, like you noticed. Another option that you might or might not find simpler would be to have a `trio.Lock`:

```python
async def sender(q, sender_lock):
    while True:
        async with sender_lock:
            batch = []
            with move_on_after(X):
                while len(batch) < Y:
                    batch.append(await q.get())

            await send_batch(batch)
```

So only one `sender` can "take charge" of collecting + sending a batch at a time.

But let's back up a moment. I'm assuming the reason you want multiple senders is because you want to increase robustness. I think there are two things we would want to watch out for: something going wonky with the `send_batch` call (e.g., the outgoing request stalling out), or the receivers piling up data faster than the senders can forward it onwards (since from what I understand of your actual problem, you don't have any way to apply backpressure to the upstream event source).

Having multiple `sender`s running could help if `send_batch` stalls out... but if it's being really flaky, and you have a fixed number of `sender`s, then eventually they'll all get stalled, which defeats the purpose.

So maybe you want something like:

```python
# A robust version of send_batch: we can kick this off, and then it will take care
# of itself, including making sure not to stall out forever
async def send_batch(batch):
    # let's do some retries in case the first try fails
    for _ in range(3):
        try:
            # And if an attempt stalls, treat that as a failure and go to the retry path
            with fail_after(10):
                ...
        except ...:
            ...
        else:
            # success!
            return
    # ...I guess all the tries failed. What should we do with the data?
    # log something and drop it on the floor, I guess?

async def sender(q):
    async with open_nursery() as nursery:
        while True:
            batch = []
            with move_on_after(X):
                while len(batch) < Y:
                    batch.append(await q.get())

            nursery.start_soon(send_batch, batch)
```

So here we might end up with several calls to `send_batch` running at the same time, but so long as your data only arrives at N items/second, then we'll only fill up a (N/Y) times/second, and `send_batch` as written above can only run for a max of 3*10 = 30 seconds, so we'll never have more than 30*N/Y simultaneous calls to `send_batch` running at a time – so as long as we choose Y appropriately we shouldn't be in any danger of running away and eating all the memory or anything.

Given that the actual goal of this batching is to avoid overloading the service you're ultimately sending it to, I wonder if it would be better to drop the maximum batch size limit entirely, and just say "we'll send out a request every X seconds, with whatever we've gathered". That slightly increases how much buffering you're doing, but presumably you can hold, like, 1 or 5 or 10 seconds worth of data in memory, and losing 1 or 5 or 10 seconds of data to a server crash isn't appreciably worse than losing 0.5 seconds or whatever it would be with the `len(batch)` limit.

If you do it like that, then you can make this even simple -- you don't even need a `Queue`, you can just use a plain list, like:

```python
async def receiver(data_to_send):
    while True:
        data_to_send.append(await get_another_item())

async def sender(data_to_send):
    async with open_nursery() as nursery:
        while True:
            await sleep(X)
            batch = data_to_send.copy()
            data_to_send.clear()
            nursery.start_soon(send_batch, batch)

async def main():
    async with open_nursery() as nursery:
        data_to_send = []
        nursery.start_soon(receiver, data_to_send)
        nursery.start_soon(sender, data_to_send)
```

What do you think?

(And returning to the original topic of this issue: I don't think any of my suggestions here would actually benefit from a `Queue.get_batch` method?)

You can use `functools.partial` instead of defining your own wrapper.

But yeah, this is intentional, and I don't see how any Python enhancements can help – the basic problem is that we need some way to distinguish between arguments that belong to `async_fn` versus arguments that belong to `run`. And right now our convention is that positional arguments belong to `async_fn` and kwargs belong to `run`.

I don't understand the motivation for having an `async_fn_args=` kwarg – if you have an iterable of positional arguments, just use `*` :-). But it's an interesting idea that we could take a `kwargs=` kwarg though ... nothing stopping us. I'm not sure if it's really more elegant than `functools.partial` though?

```python
# Door 1 (now)
trio.run(partial(some_fn, arg1, somearg=blah), clock=...)

# Door 2 (proposed)
trio.run(partial(some_fn, arg1), kwargs=dict(somearg=blah), clock=...)
```

The first line looks nicer to me, though granted you need an extra import.
I made it so that the get side automatically closes if there's no data and the put side is closed, because otherwise it would let the queue block forever.

We don't have a way to fix a released version (pypi doesn't allow resubmit another packages for a released version). The issue here is that Django 2.0 makes the on_delete a required argument to ForeignKey(). Thus, this [line](https://github.com/pyexcel-webwares/django-excel/blob/4ac31cb9f46ce0038636479814553c96d2d08d33/polls/models.py#L15) needs to be changed from:


`
    question = models.ForeignKey(Question)
`

to:

`
     question = models.ForeignKey(Question, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
`

For reference, see the [Django 2.0 docs for this example](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/intro/tutorial02/#creating-models).

针对你的测试情形，建议你配置一下自己游戏的适配规则，这样识别度会大大提高。
请查阅： [配置游戏适配规则RESIZE_METHOD](http://airtest.netease.com/docs/docs_AirtestIDE-zh_CN/4_advanced_features/1_script_record_features.html#id10)

自定义规则的resize_method函数定义（传入参数、return值请与下方定义保持一致）：
```
def custom_resize_method(w_a, h_a, resolution_a, resolution_b):
    # 根据传入的resolution_a分辨率下的宽高分别为w_a和h_a的UI元素，
    # 返回该UI元素在resolution_b下的宽w_b和高w_b
    w_b = .....(根据适配逻辑计算过程)
    h_b = .....(根据适配逻辑计算过程)
    return int(w_b), int(w_b)
```

按照文档中的描述方法把自定义函数传给RESIZE_METHOD即可生效，有效提升识别的准确度。

I strongly suspect you might have overlooked the window_length parameter in the input-shape which you define. What is the `input_shape` you define in your first layer? 
Did you put something like: 
`model.add(Something(input_shape=(1,) +(144,256,3) ))`? 
The tuple part `(1,)` here corresponds to a window_length of 1, so the agent gets one observation at a time.
